data.title: Deere, Agco face deeper slump from tariff tensions, Jefferies warns
data.description:
Agricultural equipment manufacturers such as Deere (NYSE:DE) and Agco (NYSE:AGCO) could face a prolonged downturn as renewed global trade tensions drive uncertainty in agricultural markets, according to Stephen Volkmann, equity analyst at Jefferies.

“An intensifying trade battle in ag commodities risks a longer cycle trough,” Volkmann said in an April 1 report to clients. While federal aid has helped prop up farm income in past disputes, history suggests that such payments rarely translate into spending on new machinery—bad news for capital equipment makers.

Retaliatory tariffs hit billions in U.S. exports

So far, about $27 billion in U.S. agricultural exports have been hit with retaliatory tariffs, according to Jefferies’ analysis. Countries including China, Mexico and Canada have already taken action or signaled their intention to do so, escalating tensions as the U.S. considers expanding its own tariffs in early April.

China’s retaliation includes tariffs of as much as 15% on key U.S. exports such as wheat, corn and chicken, and 10% tariffs on soybeans, pork, beef, dairy and various fruits and vegetables. Canada followed suit in March with a 25% levy on $5.8 billion of American agricultural and food products.

With global tariffs on the rise, Volkmann said broader retaliation appears likely, posing a significant headwind to U.S. ag exports.

Risk of long-term market share loss

“Tariffs make U.S. exports more expensive than other sources like Brazil, Europe and Argentina,” Volkmann said. “Once U.S. farmers lose share in global markets, it’s hard to regain.”

Foreign competitors could capitalize on the shifting trade landscape, especially Brazil, which has untapped agricultural land that could be rapidly brought into production. This structural shift could depress U.S. commodity prices over the long term, undermining farm income and equipment demand, according to Jefferies.

Government aid unlikely to help

During the last major trade dispute with China (2018–2020), the U.S. government responded with nearly $40 billion in aid to farmers through the USDA’s Market Facilitation and Commodity Credit programs. A similar strategy is already underway: the American Relief Act of 2025 has earmarked $10 billion for one-time direct payments to offset lower crop prices and rising input costs.

However, Volkmann cautioned that these funds are unlikely to drive a rebound in machinery sales.

“Farmers don’t historically spend government payments on equipment,” he said, citing muted equipment demand during the previous trade war despite rising farm income and generous subsidies.

Outlook for Deere and Agco at risk

Jefferies believes the second-half outlook for agricultural machinery manufacturers could be clouded by the growing trade dispute. Agco may face added pressure because of its reliance on European imports — roughly 25% of its U.S. machine sales come from Europe, which could be directly affected by tariffs.

Deere (NYSE:DE) is less exposed to imported equipment, but would still be vulnerable to broader market dynamics, especially if farmer sentiment remains cautious and ag commodity prices stay low.

“Government payments will help maintain farmer balance sheets,” Volkmann said, “but history shows that farmers tend not to spend bailout money on capital equipment.”

data.title: Information Technology Q1 round up: VRSN tops the chart while ON becomes worst performer
data.description:
The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLK), which tracks the S&P 500’s information technology sector, declined about 11% in the first quarter, compared to an over 4% decline in the benchmark S&P 500 Index. 

Despite high-profile names such as Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Apple (AAPL), the information technology sector did not fare well in the quarter. 

There were several reasons for the sector’s decline, including U.S. President Donald Trump’s choppy tariffs, which dampened investors’ confidence. Markets are still trying to assess the potential inflationary impact of tariffs. 

Besides tariffs, the technology sector also saw some losses during the beginning of the year after a Chinese startup announced the launch of DeepSeek. 

DeepSeek rattled the U.S. tech sector after it unveiled its AI model R1 and detailed in a paper how large language models, or LLMs, could be built on tight budgets and improved without human supervision.

AI-behemoth Nvidia (NVDA) saw around $589B in market cap erased on Jan. 27. 

Industries Q1 Performance

The Software & Services sub-sector declined more than 9% in the last quarter, while the Technology Hardware & Equipment sub-sector saw a loss of more than 8%. Meanwhile, the Semiconductor & Semiconductor Equipment industry declined more than 19% in the January-March quarter. 

U.S. stock fund flow in and out of the sector was mostly stable throughout the quarter, with an increased inflow and outflow in the months of January and March. The Information Technology-focused ETF had a net outflow of $452M. 

Top 5 movers of Q1: 

Gainers: Verisign (VRSN) +25.23%

Uber Technologies (UBER) +18.31%

Roper Technologies (ROP)  +11.88%

International Business Machines (IBM) +10.87%

Intel (INTC) +10.67%

Losers: ON Semiconductor (ON) -39.14%

Teradyne (TER) -36.90%

Arista Networks (ANET) -32.11%

Broadcom (AVGO) -31.41%

First Solar (FSLR) -31.16% 

What Quantitative Measures say?

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating has given XLK a SELL rating, with a score of 1.81 out of 5. The rating system has given the technology sector tracking fund an A+ in liquidity, and a D on momentum and risk. 

What do analysts expect?

Analysts remain optimistic, with FactSet analyst John Butters believing that the Information Technology sector is expected to see the largest price increase in the next 12 months, as it had the largest upside difference between the bottom-up target price and the closing price on March 27. 

Seeking Alpha analyst Damon Judd believes that emerging trends like AI, quantum computing, and spatial computing can drive tech growth. 

“XLK's favorable macroeconomic conditions, steady interest rates, and positive growth outlook support a Buy rating for 2025, despite potential short-term tariff impacts,” Judd added.

data.title: nCino downgraded as Wall Street reacts to disappointing earnings, guidance; stock dives
data.description:
nCino (NASDAQ:NCNO) shares tanked 34% in Wednesday premarket trading after posting soft Q1 and FY2026 guidance as well as below-consensus Q4 2025 profit, prompting Goldman Sachs and Truist Securities to downgrade the stock.

A slowdown in the banking technology platform's core commercial business, driven by internal execution issues (i.e., slower DocFox integration) and external headwinds (i.e., slower mortgage lending activity), was underestimated by Goldman, it acknowledged in a note to clients.

The Wall Street firm expects growth in nCino's (NASDAQ:NCNO) commercial lending arm will become more dependent on "new product execution and indicative of challenges in the core pipeline that we think are likely to persist near-term."

The company's new product suite, coupled with its new pricing model and a potential rebound in commercial and industrial lending, could result in overall growth reacceleration, Goldman wrote. Still, "we ultimately believe nCino is now a 'show me' story in a difficult environment that will likely need multiple quarters of execution for shares to outperform from here."

Despite the company's increased scale since its initial public offering in 2020, its ability to execute has not kept up with "the full extent of the market opportunity," CEO said during NCNO's earnings conference call. "The rapid rise in interest rates in 2022 caused the banks to pull back on spending, and the liquidity crisis in early 2023 led to even greater caution around large-scale technology investments."

Meanwhile, Truist analyst Terry Tillman also lowered his rating recommendation on nCino (NCNO) to Hold from Buy. He noted that Q4 results came in "mostly solid," though they were offset by headwinds affecting guidance, including slower-than-forecast consumer solutions growth, overly optimistic calls for a mortgage rebound, and weak sales execution.

"Despite various initiatives in play to address the headwinds, we believe the stock could remain a show-me story owing to mgmt expectation for gross bookings not to accelerate until 2HFY26 while reported resultsexpected to weaken in 2HFY26," the Wednesday note said.

The Hold ratings agree with the SA Quant system rating, but diverge from the average Wall Street analyst rating of Buy.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow drop as Trump's tariffs take center stage; private sector job growth surges
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were in the red on Wednesday, as investors braced for President Trump's reciprocal tariff plans, while U.S. private sector job growth surged in March.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was -1.2%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.4%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.8%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was down 5 basis points to 4.11%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 3 basis points to 3.84%.

March U.S. private sector employment came in at +155K vs. +120K consensus and +84K prior (revised from +77K), according to the ADP National Employment Report released on Wednesday.

Wall Street's major averages finished mixed Tuesday in the jumpstart of Q2 action, as investors waited for the Trump administration's “Liberation Day” plan to impose large-scale tariffs on trading partners worldwide.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Wednesday is slated to formally announce "country-based" tariffs during an appearance in the Rose Garden at 4 pm ET.

"We clearly don’t know any of the details, including which countries will be targeted and at what rate, with reporting yesterday suggesting that a final decision was still to be made," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

The U.S. Trade Representative's office is preparing a third tier of tariffs that would be lower than a potential 20% universal levy option, The Wall Street Journal reported Tuesday.

Ahead of today’s announcement, fears about stagflation in financial markets continued to mount, even if markets had a pretty positive day yesterday. The stagflation fears were exacerbated by the latest batch of U.S. data, Reid added. 

"The erratic nature of trade policy, uncertainty about whether legal procedures will be followed, limited clarity about end-objectives, the possibility that Trump will again retreat from some taxes, and the unpredictability of patronage-style deals to gain exemptions create uncertainty about the direct effect of the taxes," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

The second-round effects depend on unpredictable reactions by U.S. companies, a far more complex global trade system than existed 20 years ago, and U.S. consumers whose reaction to inflation has shifted, Donovan added. 

The economic calendar is on the lighter side today, with mainly February factory orders slated to land, which is expected to come in at 0.5% M/M.

data.title: 4 stocks to watch on Wednesday: BB, RH, RIVN and TKO
data.description:
U.S. stock index futures were in selloff mode on Wednesday, as market participants geared up for President Donald Trump's "Liberation Day" plan for sweeping tariffs. Here are some stocks to watch on Wednesday: 

  • U.S.-listed shares of BlackBerry (NYSE:BB) slumped -22% in pre-market trading. The Canadian technology firm's FQ4 2025 top- and bottom-line beat was overshadowed by disappointing guidance for the current quarter and fiscal year 2026. BlackBerry (NYSE:BB), which achieved worldwide fame in the early 2000s with its range of smartphones, has in recent years pivoted itself into a provider of cybersecurity products along with software services such as making operating systems used in vehicles. 
  • RH (NYSE:RH) stock will be in focus, ahead of the company's FQ4 2024 result after the closing bell. Wall Street expects the upscale home-furnishings retailer to earn $1.91 per share on revenue of $829.53M. The latter figure is seen rising 12.4% Y/Y, as the Corte Madera, Calif.-based firm continues to perform well despite operating in what top boss Gary Friedman on the FQ3 earnings call said was "the worst housing market in 30 years." Wedbush spoke highly of RH (NYSE:RH) in January. 
  • Class A shares of Rivian Automotive (NASDAQ:RIVN) were -3.1% ahead of the opening bell, despite the luxury electric vehicle (EV) maker topping Q1 deliveries expectations and backing it full-year deliveries guidance. Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) delivered 8,640 vehicles in the quarter, and reaffirmed its 2025 delivery range outlook of 46K to 51K vehicles. The EV maker last week said it would spin out its micromobility division into a new company called Also. 
  • Class A shares of TKO Group (NYSE:TKO) were +0.7% in pre-market trading. The UFC parent said the mixed martial arts organization was entering into a multiyear partnership with Meta Platforms (META). The Facebook-parent will become the UFC's official fan technology partner, collaborating on services and products. Meta (META) will also become the UFC's official artificial intelligence (AI) glasses partner, and will work to integrate the technology at UFC events.

data.title: ServiceNow appears set to maintain 20% revenue growth despite DOGE: Stifel
data.description:
Despite pockets of mid-market uncertainty, ServiceNow (NYSE:NOW) continues to generate consistent enterprise deals, which should allow the company to maintain about 20% revenue growth, according to Stifel.

"Based on recent checks, it appears that 1Q Enterprise deal activity was consistent, although commentary from our mid-market focused partners was weaker, driven by a heightened degree of uncertainty vs. last quarter," said Stifel analysts, led by Brad Reback, in an investor note. "That said, unlike the slowdown in the summer of 2022, our mid-market checks highlighted that the slowdown is less broad-based and more pronounced within certain pockets of weakness."

ServiceNow's cloud-based platform helps businesses automate and manage workflows.

"Looking forward, while partner commentary also pointed to solid enterprise deal pipelines, given a heightened degree of uncertainty given near-term risk that DOGE/Tariff could impact consumer purchasing decisions in the coming quarters we are taking a more conservative approach for the remainder of the year, and are taking down our FY25 and FY26 cRPO and subscription revenue estimates," Reback noted.

Stifel slightly lowered its full-year 2025 subscription revenue estimate to $12.98B from $13.03B, while 2026 was reduced to $15.39B from $15.64B. Current remaining performance obligation (cRPO) was lowered to $12.34B from $12.52B and to $14.66B from $15.03B, respectively.

"While management guided to a heavier 2H weighted fed year in FY25, we note that our search of ServiceNow's federal deals closed in the quarter shows flat Y/Y growth, and we note that all DoD deals have yet to be recorded and the year ago comp includes them," Reback said. "Overall given the current state of affairs we view the federal data as encouraging."

Stifel lowered its price target on ServiceNow to $950 from $1,175. It assigned a Buy rating to the stock.

ServiceNow is slated to report its first quarter 2025 financial results on April 23. A consensus estimate expects revenue of $3.09B and adjusted earnings per share of $3.83.

data.title: C3is to conduct a reverse stock split
data.description:
• C3is (NASDAQ:CISS) said it will implement a one-for-six reverse stock split.
  • The firm added the split will reduce the number of outstanding shares to 0.7M shares from 4.2M shares.
  • The split is intended to increase the market price of the stock, which will help the firm satisfy the minimum bid price requirement for maintaining listing on Nasdaq.

data.title: Stagwell appoints John Kahan as inaugural Chief AI Officer
data.description:
• Stagwell (NASDAQ:STGW) has announced the appointment of John Kahan as the network's inaugural Chief AI Officer.
  • Reporting into Mark Penn, Stagwell Chairman and CEO, Kahan will spearhead the integration and development of artificial intelligence across Stagwell's global network.
  • Kahan most recently served as Vice President and Chief Data Analytics Officer at Microsoft, where he played a fundamental role in the development of several key data platforms and insights that were integral to transforming Microsoft into the $3T company it is today.

data.title: Sidus Space jumps on $120M agreement with Lonestar for lunar data storage spacecraft
data.description:
• Sidus Space (NASDAQ:SIDU) announced on Wednesday the decision to move forward with, and broaden the scope of, a preliminary agreement valued at approximately $120M with Lonestar Data. 
  • The agreement defines the collaboration between Sidus and Lonestar to design, build and provide on-orbit support for six lunar data storage spacecrafts. 
  • As part of the contract, Sidus will oversee the design, payload integration, launch planning and on-orbit support for the spacecraft. 
  • Sidus Space (NASDAQ:SIDU) shares were up more than 23% in premarket trading.

data.title: Tesla deliveries plunged in Q1 amid the Model Y changeover and Elon Musk backlash
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) reported 336,681 deliveries for Q1 to fall short of the consensus estimate for a tally of 377,000 vehicles. The electric vehicle maker said it produced 362,615 vehicles during the quarter. Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) said 4% of the deliveries total was subject to operating lease accounting.

Model 3/Y deliveries for the quarter were 323,800, while other models accounted for 12,881 deliveries. Overall, the quarterly deliveries tally was the lowest for Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) since 2022.

"While the changeover of Model Y lines across all four of our factories led to the loss of several weeks of production in Q1, the ramp of the New Model Y continues to go well," highlighted the company

For reference, Tesla (TSLA) delivered 462,890 vehicles and 495,570 vehicles in the two preceding quarters and 386,810 vehicles in Q1 a year ago.

Looking ahead, analysts have not yet slashed their delivery forecasts for the remaining three quarters. The consensus estimates are for Q2 deliveries of 456,000, Q3 deliveries of 497,000, Q4 deliveries of 544,000. The optimistic forecasts are based on hopes that the new Model Y will be well received and that a sub-$30K model will finally be introduced. Also in the mix, full-scale production of the CyberCab could boost TSLA's deliveries numbers significantly in 2026 if demand for the two-seat robotaxi without a steering wheel takes off.

Shares of Tesla (TSLA) were down 4.8% in premarket trading. The EV stock is down 33.5% on a year-to-date basis. Short interest stands at 2.5% of the total float.
